coredata.dc:title: Characterization of Al–Ti phases in cycled TiF3-enhanced Na2LiAlH6
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Nakamura, Y.
Fossdal, A.
Brinks, H.W.
Hauback, B.C.
coredata.dc:description: TiF3-enhanced Na2LiAlH6 was investigated after dehydrogenation–hydrogenation cycles by synchrotron X-ray diffraction. There was no sign of Ti after ball-milling with TiF3, but two types of Al–Ti phases were observed in the cycled samples. In a sample after measuring five pressure–composition isotherms in the temperature range from 170 to 250°C, a fcc phase with a =3.987Å was observed. This phase is considered to be Al3Ti with the L12 structure. Samples after one or four cycles at selected temperatures between 170 and 250°C showed diffraction from another fcc phase with a ≈4.03Å. This indicates formation of an Al1−y Ti y solid-solution phase with y ≈0.15 similar to previously reported for cycled NaAlH4 with Ti additives.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Powder X-ray diffraction
Synchrotron radiation
Metal hydrides
Crystal structure
Alanates

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SR-PXD pattern for Na2LiAlH6 ball-milled with 10% TiF3 (sample 8) showing observed intensities (dots), calculated intensities (upper line) and differences (bottom line). Tick marks indicate the positions of the Bragg diffractions for Na2LiAlH6, Al and Na3AlH6 (from the top). R wp =6.38, R p =4.65, S =1.25.
Diffraction peaks of Al3Ti in the SR-PXD pattern for Na2LiAlH6 after the P–C isotherm measurements (sample 1). Observed intensities (dots) and calculated intensities (line).

SR-PXD pattern for Na2LiAlH6 after one cycle at 170°C (sample 2). Tick marks indicate the positions of the Bragg diffractions for Na2LiAlH6, Al, Al1−y Ti y (solid solution) and NaH from the top. R wp =6.14, R p =4.50, S =2.88.
SR-PXD patterns for Na2LiAlH6 after one or four cycles (samples 2–7) for 2θ =12.0–12.5° and 14.0–14.5°, focusing on the first and second diffraction peaks of Al. *: unassigned peak.

Observed intensities (dots) and calculated intensities (line) from the Rietveld refinement of Na2LiAlH6 after one cycle at (a) 170°C and (b) 230°C (samples 2 and 4) around the first and second peaks of Al. Tick marks indicate the positions of the Bragg diffractions for Na2LiAlH6, Al and Al1−y Ti y (from the top). The arrow indicates a broad and weak peak at 2θ ≈12.4–12.6°.

Observed intensities (dots) and calculated intensities (line) from the Rietveld refinement of Na2LiAlH6 after (a) one cycle for 4 days and (b) four cycles at 200°C (samples 6 and 7) around the first and second peaks of Al. Tick marks and the arrow indicate the same as in Fig. 5.
Preparing conditions for the examined samples

article.ce:title: Characterization of Al–Ti phases in cycled TiF3-enhanced Na2LiAlH6

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics, Institute for Energy Technology, P.O. Box 40, NO-2027 Kjeller, Norway

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: TiF3-enhanced Na2LiAlH6 was investigated after dehydrogenation–hydrogenation cycles by synchrotron X-ray diffraction. There was no sign of Ti after ball-milling with TiF3, but two types of Al–Ti phases were observed in the cycled samples. In a sample after measuring five pressure–composition isotherms in the temperature range from 170 to 250°C, a fcc phase with a =3.987Å was observed. This phase is considered to be Al3Ti with the L12 structure. Samples after one or four cycles at selected temperatures between 170 and 250°C showed diffraction from another fcc phase with a ≈4.03Å. This indicates formation of an Al1−y Ti y solid-solution phase with y ≈0.15 similar to previously reported for cycled NaAlH4 with Ti additives.

ce:section.ce:para: Complex Al-based hydrides, so-called ‘alanates’, have been investigated intensively as a candidate for hydrogen-storage materials with a high gravimetric capacity since reversible hydrogenation was reported for Ti-doped NaAlH4, Na3AlH6 and Na2LiAlH6 [1]. After that, dehydrogenation and re-hydrogenation of LiAlH4, Li3AlH6 [2–7], Mg(AlH4)2 [8,9] and KAlH4 [10] were investigated, but only KAlH4 was proven rechargeable.

The mixed alanate Na2LiAlH6 has been successfully synthesized by ball-milling [11–13]. It takes an ordered perovskite-type structure with an ordering of LiH6 and AlH6 octahedra [13]. Alternatively, it may be described as a fcc of AlH6 with Li in all the octahedral sites and Na in all the tetrahedral sites. This structure is similar to the high-temperature β-Na3AlH6 phase [14,15], where 1/3 of the Na is in the Li positions of Na2LiAlH6. Na2LiAlH6 with Ti additives showed only one plateau in the pressure–composition (P–C) isotherms with a reversible hydrogen capacity larger than 3wt.% (theoretical value: 3.5wt.%) [1,16]. It is dehydrogenated and re-hydrogenated in a similar way to Na3AlH6 as follows:
Na2LiAlH6 =2NaH+LiH+Al+3/2H2
Na2LiAlH6 is more stable than Na3AlH6. The dissociation enthalpy was determined as 56.4kJ/mol H2 [16], which is larger than for Na3AlH6 (∼47kJ/mol H2 [17]).

Reversibility and kinetics of the reaction of the alanates greatly depend on catalysts. A number of catalysts have been tested [1,17–21]. The results indicate that Ti-based additives are most effective. However, how the additives work is still not understood. In the NaAlH4 system, a lot of efforts have been done for clarifying the mechanism of Ti [22–29]. No species containing Ti was detected in as-milled samples (before dehydrogenation) by diffraction [25,26]. After 7 cycles of NaH+Al with 2% Ti(OBu)4 [25], 100 cycles of NaAlH4 with 2% Ti(OBu)4 and 3 cycles of NaAlH4 with 10% TiCl3 [29], diffraction from a fcc phase with a unit-cell dimension slightly smaller than Al was observed. This phase was assigned to an Al1−y Ti y solid-solution phase, with y <0.25. The fraction of Ti in this phase was estimated to y ≈0.07 based on the unit cell volume [25], and from the more accurate quantitative phase analysis, y ≈0.15 was found [29].

In this study, formation of Al–Ti phases during dehydrogenation–hydrogenation cycles was investigated for TiF3-enhanced Na2LiAlH6 by synchrotron radiation powder X-ray diffraction (SR-PXD). Four series of samples were examined: (1) samples after ball-milling with TiF3, (2) a sample after measuring five P–C isotherms at 170–250°C, (3) samples after one cycle at selected temperatures between 170 and 250°C, and (4) samples after four cycles and one cycle spending 4 days at 200°C. Comparison of the results for the samples in (3) will give the temperature dependence of the phase formation, and the differences among (2), (3) and (4) will provide the dependence on preparing process and the cycling spending times.

Na2LiAlH6 after P–C isotherm measurements at five temperatures (170, 200, 210, 230 and 250°C), which totally took 7 weeks, was examined as sample 1. The synthesis and measurement conditions are described in Ref. [16].

The other samples were synthesized as follows: a mixture of NaAlH4 (Albemarle, recrystallized before use), NaH (Sigma–Aldrich) and LiH (Sigma–Aldrich) with a molar ratio of 1:2:1 was ball-milled at a speed of 700rpm for 3h. The milled sample was then subjected to 80bar of hydrogen at 150°C overnight (for ∼14h). The synthesized sample was confirmed to be nearly single phase, with only a trace of Na3AlH6. Before cycling, the sample was mixed with 2mol% TiF3 by ball-milling for 20min at 350rpm. Then, the sample was split into six portions. Four of them were dehydrogenated and re-hydrogenated (one cycle) at 170, 200, 230 and 250°C, respectively (samples 2–5). The samples were held in a hydrogen pressure lower than 1bar for 2–5h during dehydrogenation, and subjected to hydrogen at the initial pressure of 80bar for ∼16h during hydrogenation. The fifth sample was cycled once at 200°C spending 4 days (sample 6), and the sixth sample was cycled four times at 200°C (sample 7). A sample with 10mol% TiF3 was also synthesized in the same way as samples 2–7 before cycling (sample 8). All operations were performed under argon atmosphere with <1ppm oxygen and water. The preparing conditions for the samples described above are listed in Table 1 .

SR-PXD data at 22°C was collected at the Swiss–Norwegian beam line (station BM01B) at the European Synchrotron Radiation Facility (ESRF) in Grenoble, France. The samples were contained in rotating boron–silica–glass capillaries with 0.5-mm diameter. The data was collected in steps of Δ(2θ)=0.003° to 0.012°. The wavelength was 0.51979Å (for sample 1) and 0.49957Å (for samples 2–8).
The obtained SR-PXD data were analyzed by the Rietveld refinement programs Fullprof version 2.50 [30] (for sample 1) and RIETAN-2000 [31] (for the other data).

SR-PXD data for Na2LiAlH6 with 10% TiF3 (sample 8), cf. Fig. 1 , clearly shows formation of Al, but no Ti- or F-containing species were detected. This is analogous to NaAlH4 where 6% and 10% TiF3 gave the same result [25,29]. The Ti-containing phase is either amorphous or has a small crystallite size below the detection limit of SR-PXD (about 5nm). No NaF or LiF were found in any of the samples studied, even after cycling. A small amount of Na3AlH6 was also detected (∼4.8wt.%), probably due to incomplete reaction to Na2LiAlH6 in the sample preparation.

The sample after P–C isotherm measurements at five temperatures between 170 and 250°C (sample 1) was investigated by SR-PXD. Compared to the as-prepared sample [16], extra peaks were observed at the high-angle side of the Al peaks (Fig. 2 ). This cubic phase with relative intensities as a fcc phase has a unit-cell dimension a =3.978(1) Å. This is smaller than for the Al0.85Ti0.15 shoulder (a =4.0365Å) earlier observed in cycled Ti-enhanced NaAlH4 [25,29]. For comparison, a =4.049Å for Al. The present unit-cell dimension agrees well with earlier reports of Al3Ti in the metastable L12 structure modification (a =4.00Å) [32,33].

This L12 modification of Al3Ti is reported to be only partially ordered [32,33]. It is difficult to distinguish order or disorder structure from the diffraction pattern because intensities of the L12 ordering diffraction peaks are so small: even for a complete L12 ordering the intensities of L12 ordering peaks are not larger than 8.7% of the strongest peak of Al3Ti (by calculation). Thus, whether this is a partially ordered phase (L12 structure) or a completely disordered phase (solid solution) with around 25% Ti could not be unambiguously confirmed. However, based on the unit-cell dimension the extra phase observed in sample 1 was considered to be Al3Ti with the L12 structure.

There are several reports about formation of Al3Ti phase during the reaction between alanates and Ti catalysts. Haber et al. reported that Al3Ti with the D022 structure formed from the reaction between LiAlH4 and TiCl3 in boiling mesitylene and subsequent heating the products to 550°C [34]. Balema et al. reported that ball-milling LiAlH4 with TiCl4 for 10min provided Al3Ti with the D022 structure and the Al3Ti transformed from the D022 structure to the L12 structure (a =3.97Å) by ball-milling for 8h [3]. Formation of Al3Ti by ball-milling of a mixture of NaAlH4 and TiCl3 in a 3:1 ratio was reported by Majzoub and Gross [27]. The L12 ordering diffractions were not observed in these cases, but the phase was assigned to the L12 structure modification of Al3Ti based on the observed cubic unit cell rather than the tetragonal unit cell as for Al3Ti with the D022 structure.

From previous experiments for NaAlH4, it is known that metastable Al1−y Ti y with y ≈0.15 formed during the first three cycles and it remains stable at least up to 100 cycles carried out at 120–160°C [25,29]. Seven weeks of heat treatment at 170–250°C for Na2LiAlH6 under various hydrogen pressures results in formation of the more stable Al3Ti. In order to achieve a better understanding of the effect of temperature and time of heat treatment on the Al–Ti formation, a number of samples were synthesized.

The temperature effect was investigated for samples 2–5. They were cycled one time at a temperatures ranging from 170 to 250°C. In these samples, NaH was detected as a minor phase by SR-PXD as shown in Fig. 3 (e.g. 2.6wt.% in sample 2), indicating incomplete rehydrogenation. Fig. 4 shows that shoulders on the high-angle side of Al are present. From the refinements this was assigned to a fcc phase with a unit-cell dimension ranging from 4.025 to 4.029Å (Fig. 5 ). This is slightly smaller than earlier observed for Al0.85Ti0.15 (4.0362, 4.0368 and 4.0344Å) in NaAlH4 [25,29]. This difference could at least partially be due to small deviations in the refinements, but there may also be a real difference in composition. Anyway, the obtained result indicates that the Al1−y Ti y phase forms not only in the NaAlH4 system, but also in the Na2LiAlH6 system. It should also be emphasized that the formation was confirmed after only one cycle in the Na2LiAlH6 system.

The formation of Al1−y Ti y observed for the cycled Na2LiAlH6 is independent of the temperature during cycling. From this, it is clear that Al1−y Ti y is not transformed into Al3Ti at least for short heat treatments.

In order to investigate the dependence of the time of heat treatment on Al–Ti formation, sample 6 and 7 were prepared. Both samples were kept at 200°C for four days during cycling. They differ by the number of cycles, 1 and 4, respectively. A fcc phase with a =4.030 and 4.029Å was observed (Figs. 4 and 6 ) indicating formation of the Al1−y Ti y solid-solution phase with a Ti content similar to that obtained for samples 2–5. In addition, as indicated in Fig. 6(b), very broad and weak peak was observed at 2θ ≈12.4–12.6°. Similar broad peak is observed also in Fig. 5(b). Assuming that this comes from another fcc phase, the lattice parameter would be a ≈4.00Å. This could be a preliminary state of an Al–Ti phase with a higher Ti content than 15% before becoming well crystallized.

From the above results, formation of two types of Al–Ti phase was found in cycled TiF3-doped Na2LiAlH6: (i) an Al1−y Ti y solid-solution phase with y ≈0.15 and (ii) an Al3Ti phase in the L12 structure modification. The metastable Al1−y Ti y phase is formed already after one cycle in all the attempts. Even sample 1 containing excess Al formed Al3Ti instead of the more Al-rich Al0.85Ti0.15. The reason for this is probably that Al3Ti is more stable than Al0.85Ti0.15 and that long heat treatments at higher temperatures results in transformation to Al3Ti (cf. the cycling temperature for NaAlH4, with formation of only Al0.85Ti0.15, was ≤160°C [25,29]). Structurally, Al3Ti with the L12 structure is more similar to Al1−y Ti y solid solution than Al3Ti with the D022 structure, and therefore probably is kinetically more easily formed.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported from the project ‘Hydrogen Storage System for Automotive Applications – STORHY’ (Contract No. SES6-CT-2004-502667) under the European Commission's Sixth Framework Program, and by the Research Council of Norway, Grant No. 158516/S10 (NANOMAT). The skillful assistance from the project team at the Swiss–Norwegian beam line, ESRF, is also gratefully acknowledged.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: A. Fossdal, H.W. Brinks, M. Fichtner, B.C. Hauback, J. Alloys Compd., in press.
